title: The Old Saint And Three Questions

content.problemStatement:
Once upon a time, there was a hero and an old saint. And like in any story with a hero and an old saint, the old saint asked the hero — three questions!

But here's the twist: each question was a **binary** question, which means that the answer to each must be either a 'Yes' or a 'No', not none, not both. Our hero, who was not so wise in the ways of science, answered them arbitrarily and just hoped he is correct. The old saint, being so old, does not remember which answers were correct. The only thing that he remembers is - how many of them were 'Yes', and how many of them were 'No'. Our hero will pass the test if the old saint cannot distinguish his responses from the **set** of correct answers i.e. if the number of 'Yes' and 'No' in the responses matches that in the correct answers, regardless of their order.

You are given the answers to each of the three questions, and the responses of the hero to the same. Find whether the hero will be able to pass the old saint's test.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, the number of test cases. The description of the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case consists of three space-separated integers A1A_1A1​ A2A_2A2​ A3A_3A3​, representing the correct answers to the first, second, and third question respectively (000 for 'No', 111 for 'Yes').
The second line of each test case consists of three space-separated integers B1B_1B1​ B2B_2B2​ B3B_3B3​, representing the response of the hero to the first, second, and third question respectively (000 for 'No', 111 for 'Yes').


Output Format
For each test case, print "Pass" (without quotes) if the hero passes the old saint's test, "Fail" (without quotes) otherwise.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains three space‑separated integers A1 A2 A3 (each 0 or 1) – the correct answers. The second line contains three space‑separated integers B1 B2 B3 (each 0 or 1) – the hero's answers.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line: "Pass" if the multiset of answers of the hero matches the multiset of correct answers (i.e., the number of 1's is the same), otherwise output "Fail".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: correct answers have two Yes (1) and one No (0); hero's answers also have two Yes and one No, so the saint cannot distinguish – Pass. Test case 2: correct answers have zero Yes, hero's answers have three Yes – counts differ, so Fail.
Both correct and hero answers contain exactly one Yes and two No, therefore the hero passes.
Correct answers have three Yes, hero's answers have two Yes – mismatch, so Fail.

solution.approach: For each test case count the number of 1's (Yes) in the correct answer triple and in the hero's answer triple. If the two counts are equal, the numbers of Yes and No answers match, so the saint cannot distinguish and the hero "Passes"; otherwise he "Fails". This requires only O(1) work per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each of the T test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables are used.

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        a1 = int(next(it)); a2 = int(next(it)); a3 = int(next(it))
        b1 = int(next(it)); b2 = int(next(it)); b3 = int(next(it))
        cntA = a1 + a2 + a3
        cntB = b1 + b2 + b3
        out.append('Pass' if cntA == cntB else 'Fail')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
